coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent N-doped carbon dots for both cellular imaging and highly-sensitive catechol detection
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Hao
Kong, Weiqian
Liu, Juan
Liu, Naiyun
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) obtained by one-step reflux treatment of C3N4 with ethane diamine possess good water dispersibility, strong visible fluorescence, low toxicity, proton adsorption capacity, and photo-induced electron-accepting/donating abilities. Based on these unique properties, we demonstrate that the obtained NCDs can be used as a good fluorescent probe for cellar imaging without any further functionalization. Further, the NCDs are combined with laccase (LAC) to form NCDs–laccase (NCD–LAC) hybrids, which are sensitive, stable, and of low cost for the precise detection of catechol and catechol derivatives.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of the fluorescence quantum yields
Proton adsorption and photo-induced electron donating/accepting properties
NCD–LAC hybrid and its detection application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Typical TEM image of NCDs. (b) The size distribution of NCDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of NCDs. (b) FTIR spectrum of NCDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) XPS full-scan spectrum of NCDs. High-resolution C 1s (b), N 1s (c), and O 1s (d) XPS spectra of NCDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL excitation (black) and emission (red) spectrum of the NCDs in aqueous solution; insets are the digital photos of NCDs under visible and UV (365nm) light. (b) The PL spectra of NCDs with different excitation wavelengths in the water. (c) The effects of different pH on the normalized fluorescence intensity of NCDs. (d) Absorbance-based viability assay to HeLa cells exposed to different concentrations of NCDs. (e) The optical microscopy image of stained HeLa cell in the solution of NCDs with PB. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Dependence of contact time on removal of H+ by NCDs. (b) Luminescence decays (485nm excitation, monitored with 550nm narrow band-pass filter) of the NCDs with 2,4-dinitrotoluene and DEA, respectively. Inset: Stern–Volmer plots for the quenching of luminescence quantum yields (485nm excitation) of the NCDs by DEA. (c) After protonation, Stern–Volmer plots for the quenching of luminescence quantum yields (485nm excitation) of the NCDs by 2,4-dinitrotoluene. (d) After protonation, Stern–Volmer plots for the quenching of luminescence quantum yields (485nm excitation) of the NCDs by DEA. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic illustration from catechol to 1,2-benzoquinone during the detection process. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of 1,2-benzoquinone. (c) The superposed graphs of 1,2-benzoquinone UV spectrum and PL spectrum of NCDs. (d) The fluorescence spectra of NCDs (black line), NCDs in the presence of 0.0087mM catechol (NCD–catechol, red line), NCDs in the presence of 25μgmL−1 LAC (NCD–LAC, blue line), and NCDs in the presence of a mixture containing 25μgmL−1 LAC and 0.0087mM catechol (NCD–LAC–catechol, green line). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The normalized fluorescence spectra of NCDs containing 25μgmL−1 LAC upon addition of different concentrations of catechol from top to bottom. (b) The linear response of the quenching efficiency I 0/I of the NCDs versus concentrations of catechol. (c) The effects of guest molecules (10mM) to 0.026mM catechol on the PL of NCDs containing 25μgmL−1 LAC. (d) Analytical results of catechol in solution using the present NCD-based method (black) and using HPLC (red). All measurements were performed in PB, pH=6.8, and thermostated at 35°C for 40min. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The detection limit and detection range for the different test substances.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent N-doped carbon dots for both cellular imaging and highly-sensitive catechol detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, 199 Ren’ai Road, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices
Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) obtained by one-step reflux treatment of C3N4 with ethane diamine possess good water dispersibility, strong visible fluorescence, low toxicity, proton adsorption capacity, and photo-induced electron-accepting/donating abilities. Based on these unique properties, we demonstrate that the obtained NCDs can be used as a good fluorescent probe for cellar imaging without any further functionalization. Further, the NCDs are combined with laccase (LAC) to form NCDs–laccase (NCD–LAC) hybrids, which are sensitive, stable, and of low cost for the precise detection of catechol and catechol derivatives.

ce:section.ce:para: For highly sensitive bioimaging and biosensing, fluorescent carbon dots with tunable emission are promising alternatives to fluorescent semiconductor nanocrystals, which are composed of toxic heavy metals such as cadmium [1–5]. In order to enrich the potential applications, the doping of other elements into carbon dots is the efficient method of tuning electronic structures. Nitrogen (N), as a general dopant element within carbon materials, has a comparable atomic size and strong valence bonds with carbon atoms. The presence of the additional lone pairs of electrons and the commonly introduced defects sites from N doping would also provide possible active sites and modify the local chemical activities of carbon nanostructures. In the N-doped carbon structure, the N dopant can inject electrons into carbon-based materials, and then change the electronic and transport properties [6–11]. Based on the benefits of N doping in carbon nanostructures, it can be extrapolated that the introduction of N into carbon dots (N-doped carbon dots, NCDs) will further enhance their versatile properties, which is extremely important both fundamentally and technologically. However, it is hard to obtain many uniform and ordered NCDs by a simple method. Moreover, studies related to chemical/physical properties and the potential application of these NCDs are still scarce.

Herein, we developed a one-pot route to synthesize water-dispersible NCDs at a relatively low reaction temperature, using C3N4 and ethane diamine as precursors. As ethylene diamine is corrosive, it can degrade C3N4 to form NCDs at 80°C (the synthesis process in shown in Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. S2, it is easily observed that there are many differences between C3N4 and NCDs in terms of the XPS spectra. According to the above results, the peak of CN decreases and new peaks appear at higher binding energy, which indicates that a part of the CN bond is broken and oxidized by ethylene diamine under an atmosphere of air [12]. These NCDs exhibit good water dispersibility, strong visible fluorescence, low toxicity, and strong proton adsorption capacity, and they can also be regarded as both electron acceptors and donors. Based on these merits, NCDs can be used as a good fluorescent probe for cellular imaging. We also combined NCDs with laccase (LAC) to form the NCDs–laccase (NCDs–LAC) hybrid, which is a highly efficient, sensitive, and low-cost fluorescent probe used for the detection of catechol and catechol derivatives.
All the chemicals were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich and Beijing Chemical Reagent (Beijing, China), and they were used as received. The LAC (5.43 U/mg) was purchased from USA Sigma Co. Ltd.

The C3N4 power was obtained by directly heating urea (10g urea in a 30mL crucible) in a muffle furnace in air under certain calcination parameter settings as follows: from room temperature (RT) to 550°C, the heating rate was 10°C·min−1; it was finally heated at 550°C for 3h [13].

To 10mL of ethane diamine, 0.02g C3N4 was added to form a suspension. Then the mixture solution was refluxed for 12h at 80°C. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 7 with HCl. Finally, a golden yellow solution was obtained. To remove the impurities, the raw solution was given treated with dialysis using a semipermeable membrane (MWCO 1000). The change in color of the solution to maple implied that the pure amorphous NCDs were obtained.

All solid samples were dried under vacuum at 60°C for 8–10h before the measurement. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of fluorescent nanoparticles (FCNPs) was obtained with a Bruker Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Hyperion). The photoluminescence (PL) study was carried out on a Horiba Jobin Yvon (Fluoro Max-4) luminescence spectrometer, while ultraviolet (UV)–visible (vis) spectra were obtained with a Perkin Elmer UV–vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained with a FEI/Philips Tecnai G2 F20 TWIN TEM. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured using a KRATOS Axis ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectroscope. The fluorescence lifetimes of the NCDs was measured by a Jobin Yvon Time-correlated Single Photon Counting System (Horib-fm-2015). Laser-scanning confocal fluorescence (Leica, TCS-SP5) was used for fluorescent cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the fluorescence quantum yields
ce:section.ce:para:
Quantum yield (QY) was measured according to established procedure using quinine sulfate in 0.10M H2SO4 solution as the standard. The absorbance was measured on a Perkin Elmer UV–vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750). Absolute values were calculated according to the following equation:
Q =
Q
R
m
m
R
n
2
n
R
2
where Q is the QY, m was the slope of the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance, and n is the refractive index. The subscript refers to the reference fluorophore, quinine sulfate solution. In order to minimize the reabsorption effects, absorbance in the 1-cm quartz cuvette was kept below 0.15 [14].

All of the PL spectra were recorded on the same fluorescence spectrophotometer, and at the fixed excitation wavelength of 390nm at a scan rate of 5nm/s. For the detection of dopamine, 2mL of NCDs was mixed with 20mL of LAC solution (0.25μg/mL); the different concentrations of dopamine solutions were added into 2mL of the NCD–LAC mixture, and then the mixed solution was diluted with phosphate-buffered (PB) saline to 4mL incubated at 35°C for 35min. Finally, the mixture was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Due to the excellent water solubility of NCDs, the adsorption experiments were conducted using a dialysis method. Typically, the NCDs solution was dialyzed using a semipermeable membrane (MWCO 1000) in a 600-mL beaker, and the dialysate was 0.005M HCl (500mL). Notably, the NCDs were treated previously by the dialysis method before use, so that the NCDs would not dialyze out of the semipermeable membrane. If NCDs show good adsorption behavior of H+, H+ would gradually cross the semipermeable membrane and dialyze into the solution of NCDs. After stirring on a shaker for predetermined time intervals, the residual concentration of HCl solution was determined by titrating with 0.005M NaOH solution.

The HeLa cell line was obtained from the Cell Bank of Chinese Academy of Science and cultured in the standard medium at 37°C in 5% CO2. Cells were seeded in a 96-well plate for 24h. Serial dilutions of NCDs with known concentrations were added to the cells. After 24-h incubation, the relative viabilities of cell samples were determined by colorimetric 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays to assess the metabolic activity of cells treated as described above. The cells were lysed with acidulated sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS). Absorbance was measured at 570nm using a microplate reader (Bio-Rad 680, USA). All measurements were carried out in triplicate, and at least three independent experiments were carried out.

Fig. 1 a shows the TEM image of the obtained NCDs, from which we can see that these small sized NCDs are well dispersed. The typical size distribution of the NCDs is shown in Fig. 1b, which suggests that their diameters are in the range of 2–7nm.

Fig. 2 a depicts the typical UV–vis absorption spectrum of NCDs. The absorption band peaks at the 240–300nm region, and the absorption peak is attributed to the π–π* transition corresponding to carbon core CC units, whereas the shoulder at ∼350nm is due to the n–π* transition corresponding to the carbonyl/amine functional groups on the surface [14]. The FTIR spectrum Fig. 2b) is obtained to determine the surface functional groups of the obtained NCDs, and the result is shown in Fig. 2b: the characteristic features of the condensed C–N heterocycles, that is, the typical breathing mode of the tri-s-triazine units at 806.5cm−1 as well as its stretching mode in the 1200–1650cm−1 regions. The obtained NCDs exhibit characteristic absorption bands of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations of amine groups at 3452.4 and 3251.8cm−1 and N–H bending vibrations at 1633.6cm−1, and the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COC in the carboxylate groups are also detected at about 1037cm−1 [15]. Specifically, the bands at 1347 and 1261cm−1 respectively correspond to the stretching vibrations of the connected units of N−(C)3 (full condensation) and CNC (partial condensation) [16]. All of the above FTIR results indicate that the surfaces of NCDs are full of hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl and carboxylic groups), which coincide with the excellent aqueous dispersibility.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out to probe the composition of as-prepared NCDs, and the full-scan spectrum of NCDs is shown in Fig. 3 a. The peaks located at 283.04, 399.05 and 530.05eV correspond to C 1s of sp2 C, N 1s of the doped N, and O 1s of the oxygen functional groups, respectively. In addition, the sample contains 68.4 at.% of carbon, 17.8 at.% of oxygen, and 13.7 at.% of nitrogen at the corresponding binding energies given in Fig. 3a. The partial XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 3b) can be deconvoluted into four component peaks with binding energies at about 283.14, 284.43, 286.16, and 287.81eV, respectively. Here, the anterior peak located at 283.14eV reflects the bonding structure of C–H bonds, the second peak located at 287.81eV reflects the bonding structure of the CN/CO bonds, and the peaks at 284.40 and 286.16eV are attributed to the sp2 C–C and C–O bonds, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3c, the partial XPS spectra of N1s, similarly, following the fitting peak, the N1s peak can also be resolved into three components centered at 398.07, 399.55 and 401.1eV; 399.55 (the tertiary N bonded to carbon atoms in the form of N–(C)3 or H–N–(C)2); [17] and 398.07eV (CNC). The peak located at 401.1eV can be ascribed to ONC. The high-resolution O1s spectrum is shown in Fig. 3d. A broad peak between 528 and 535eV could be deconvoluted into three peaks. A CO/NO signal appeared at 529.79eV, whereas the other peaks are oxygen in the functional groups of partially oxidized NCDs, corresponding to C–O/N–O at 530.69eV and CO/NO at 531.69eV [17–28]. At the same time, we also tested the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum of NCDs. The experimental data reveal that the NCDs really contain the C, N, and O elements, and the sample contains 66.38 at.% of carbon, 17.4 at.% of oxygen, and 16.22 at.% of nitrogen at the corresponding binding energies given in Fig. S3. This results obtained by the EDX matched well with XPS (C: 68.4 at.%; O: 17.8 at.%; and N: 13.7 at.%) shown in Fig. 3a.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the NCDs show bright blue fluorescence with a maximum excitation wavelength and a maximum emission wavelength of 390 and 480nm, respectively. The inset image in Fig. 4a indicates that the solution of NCDs can emit bright blue-green light when it is irradiated using a UV lamp (365nm). This bright blue-green fluorescence of NCDs is strong enough to be easily seen with the naked eye. Fig. 4b shows the PL spectra of NCDs with different excitation wavelengths in water. The strongest emission peak is located at 480nm with an excitation wavelength of 390nm. With an increase in the excitation wavelengths from 350 to 430nm, the emission spectra from NCDs are gradually red-shifted to higher wavelengths accompanied by decreased fluorescence intensity, which is similar to those reported previously [29–33]. In the following PL test, the detailed PL spectra of NCDs in PB (pH=6.8) are also investigated at different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. S4, in PB, the optimal excitation wavelength is obtained at 390nm with the strongest emission peak at 470nm. Further, the QY of NCDs is about 28.5% using quinine sulfate (0.10M H2SO4 as solvent; QY=0.54) as a reference. This value is higher than other kinds of non-NCDs [32]. At the same time, the QY of NCDs is also higher ∼20% higher than N-doped carbon dots [34]. The above results indicate that the NCDs possess stable and strong fluorescence in PB solution, which may serve as an excellent fluorescent probe for biomedical imaging and bio-detection.

Fig. 4c reveals the effect of different pH on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs, and its fluorescence intensity is pH dependent. Under strongly acidic (pH<6.0) or weakly alkaline (pH>7.0) condition, the PL of NCDs exhibits a minimal change. On the other hand, the NCDs show the strongest PL emission intensity when the pH value is 7.0, which is similar to the physiological pH environment (pH=7–8). This results in NCDs possessing excellent fluorescence properties, and they can be applied in the biological field. In the acidic solution condition, the PL of NCDs undergoes little change. In other words, the PL of NCDs cannot be affected by protonation. Therefore, NCDs could be regarded as good candidates for cell imaging and potential biological detection applications.

We tested the in vitro cytotoxicity of NCDs using the HeLa cell line. As shown in Fig. 4d, we observed the metabolic activity of HeLa cells treated with different concentrations of NCDs. Varied concentrations of NCDs were added to the cells cultured in 96-well plates and incubated for 48h. Subsequently, a standard assay was performed to assess the cell viabilities after the treatment of NCDs. No significant reduction in cell viability was observed for cells treated with NCDs even at ultrahigh concentrations (up to 400μg/mL), demonstrating that the NCDs produced by our method are not obviously toxic in vitro (Fig. 4d).

The prepared NCDs are used as an ideal fluorescent probe for cell imaging based on their low toxicity, water solubility, small particle size, and high luminescence efficiency. Fig. 4e shows the confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells incubated with NCDs (the concentration is 200μg/mL) for 3h at 37°C. Under the 488nm excitation [5], bright luminescence of NCDs in the cell cytoplasm region is observed using a laser-scanning confocal microscope. The results indicate that the NCDs are considerably biocompatible as bioimaging agents, and they could be used as an excellent optical imaging probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proton adsorption and photo-induced electron donating/accepting properties
ce:section.ce:para:
To testify the proton (H+) adsorption capacity of NCDs which can affect the detection of catechol, the H+ adsorption experiments of NCDs are investigated. Because NCDs have many functional groups, such as –OH, –COOH, and –CO, on the surface and the NCDs contain many N atoms, the H+ is drawn closer to the surface of NCDs. At the same time, NCDs contain the N element, which is helpful for enhancing the adsorption capacity [34,35]. On account of the excellent water solubility of NCDs, the adsorption experiments are conducted (see experimental section for details). The amount of adsorbed H+ (based on the quality of HCl), Q (mg/g) is calculated by the following equation:
Q =
(
C
0
-
C
e
) · V
1000
W
where C 0 and C e are the initial and equilibrium concentration (mg/L), respectively; V is the volume of HCl solution (mL) and W is the weight (g) of NCD adsorbent. Fig. 5 a shows the dependence of contact time on removal of H+ by NCDs, from which the adsorption of H+ is extraordinarily rapid in the first 30min, then gradually increasing with prolongation of the contact time. After 1h of adsorption, the amount of H+ remains constant, which suggests that 1h is the equilibrium time in this adsorption experiment. In addition, the amount of adsorbed H+ (based on the quality of HCl) is calculated to be about 162.4mg/g for NCDs. These results indicate that NCDs possess absorption capacity of H+, which could promote the detection of catechol and shorten the testing time of catechol.

The followed experiments indicate that NCDs could act as both electron donors and electron acceptors under visible light. Fig. S5 and 5b show the luminescence decays (485nm excitation, monitored with a 550nm narrow band-pass filter) of NCDs, which are quenched by the known electron donor N,N-diethylaniline (DEA, 0.88V vs. NHE) and electron acceptor 2,4-dinitrotoluene (−0.9V vs. NHE), with the observed Stern–Volmer quenching constants (K sv = τF°kq) from the linear regression of 20.5 and 35.6M−1, respectively [36–38]. The above results imply that the PL of NCDs can be quenched highly efficiently by either electron acceptors or electron donors clearly, which confirms that NCDs are excellent as both electron acceptors and electron donors. After the NCDs are protonated, their electron-accepting and electron-donating capacity change. Notably, the electron-donating capacity decreases by 38.5% (K sv =12.6M−1), and the electron-accepting capacity increases by 22.2% (K sv =43.5M−1), as shown in Fig. 5c and d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NCD–LAC hybrid and its detection application
ce:section.ce:para: In the following studies, we combine NCDs with the LAC to form NCD–LAC hybrids. As a fluorescent probe, NCD–LAC is highly efficient, sensitive, stable, precise, and of low cost for the detection of catechol and catechol derivatives. In the present system (LAC–catechol), 1,2-benzoquinone produced from the LAC-catalyzed oxidation will be converted by a rapid spontaneous auto-oxidation to 1,2-benzoquinone. Moreover, during the catechol oxidation catalyzed by LAC, as shown in Fig. 6 a, H2O is also produced. Thus, efficient practical fluorescent biological probes should satisfy the important condition that the emission spectroscopy of the biosensor must match the absorption spectrum of 1,2-benzoquinone (products of oxidation by the enzyme). The above results indicate that NCDs possess this condition, which make NCDs more sensitive to catechol detection.

The above results indicate that NCDs possess the absorption capacity of H+ and photo-induced electron-donating/accepting properties. On the other hand, Fig. 6c reveals, with excitation at 390nm, that the NCDs shows the emission peak from 400 to 650nm and the strongest emission at about 480nm. Simultaneously, the absorbent peak of 1,2-benzoquinone is observed from 350 to 600nm and the strongest absorption at 420nm (as shown in Fig. 6b). Therefore, the PL of NCDs can be quenched efficiently by 1,2-benzoquinone in the presence of LAC. To prove that the PL of NCDs can be efficiently quenched by 1,2-benzoquinone, the fluorescence spectrum of different samples are performed. Fig. 6d shows the fluorescence spectra of NCDs (black line), NCDs in the presence of 0.0087mM catechol (NCDs–catechol, red line), NCDs in the presence of 25μgmL−1 LAC (NCD–LAC, blue line), and NCDs in the presence of a mixture containing 25μgmL−1 LAC and 0.0087mM catechol (NCDs–LAC–catechol, green line). As shown in Fig. 6d, the PL of NCDs is almost the same after mixing with catechol (red line) and LAC (blue line), respectively. However, when the catechol are in the presence of a mixture containing LAC and NCDs, the fluorescence of NCDs is quenched efficiently (green line). The results demonstrate that the 1,2-benzoquinone can efficiently quench the PL of NCDs and the NCDs-LAC hybrids can be applied in catechol detection.

It should be noted that, in the present hybrid probe system, many assay conditions would affect the detection results. To determine the optimal detection conditions for catechol, different parameters, such as enzymatic factor, temperature, pH value, and incubation time, were investigated. First, different concentrations of enzyme were selected, and when the concentration of LAC was 25μgmL−1, the quenching effects were observed to reach the maximum as shown in Fig. S6a. The optimal pH, temperature, and response time on the quenched PL efficiency in the presence of 0.031mM catechol are also discussed. Fig. S6b shows that, when the pH is changed from 5.0 to 8.0, the ratio I 0/I is different, and the optimum pH is 6.8. The pH of 6.8 is chosen to be the favorable pH for the detection of catechol as the quenching fluorescence intensity of NCDs is best at this pH. Fig. S6c reveals that the quenching effect reaches the maximum at 35°C. Therefore, the optimal incubation temperature is 35°C. Fig. S6d reveals that when the incubation time reaches 40min, the signal response sustains a stable value, so the optimal incubation time is 40min. Therefore, all further detection experiments are performed at 35°C, pH=6.8, and an incubation time of 40min.

In the following experiments, under the optimal detection condition (35°C, pH=6.8, and incubation time of 40min), the quantitative detection of catechol is carried out. In the present system, when catechol is detected by a solution containing NCDs and 25μgmL−1 LAC, the PL intensity of NCDs is the difference between the absence and presence of analytes changing linearly with different concentrations. Therefore, the plot of catechol exhibits good linearity. Fig. 7 a shows that the PL of NCDs is quenched under the concentrations of catechol from 3.44×10−4 to 2.66×10−6 molL−1. The detection limit of catechol is tested at 3.00×10−7 molL−1. The ratio I 0/I (I 0 and I are the PL intensities of NCDs in the absence and presence of analytes, respectively) is proportional to the catechol concentration. Fig. 7a and b shows that the PL of NCDs is quenched under different concentrations of catechol with the linear regression equation being I 0/I =0.92807+19890.07C catechol (R 2 =0.9997). The lowest detection limit and wide detection range prove that the NCDs are an excellent candidate for fabrication of PL biosensors for a variety of phenolic compounds.

In order to further evaluate the specificity of the developed biosensor for catechol, many kinds of metal ions, anions, amino acids, carbohydrate, phenol, and cresol are selected. Fig. 7c shows that, for glucose, levulose, lactose and maltose, I 0/I displays a minor influence on catechol detection. For phenol and cresol, the results also exhibit minimal influence on catechol detection even at a concentration 10 times higher than that of catechol, intimating the high specification. However, the amino acids, typical metal ions, and some anions show minimal effect in catechol determination. These experimental results show that the NCDs can serve as new fluorescence probes for reliable and highly selective catechol monitoring. In order to investigate the accuracy of this detected method, all measurements were performed in PB, pH=6.8, and incubated at 35°C for 40min. Samples 1–5 are the five different samples with different catechol concentrations. Meanwhile, the five different concentration samples are also detected by the high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method. It can be seen that the results obtained by the two detection methods are matched well shown in Fig. 7d and their relative error (fluorescence detection vs. HPLC detection) is −1.56% to −10%, as shown in Table S1. Table S1 presents a comparison between the HPLC method and the fluorescence method for the values of five different catechol concentration samples. The above results reveal that the fluorescence method for determination of catechol in this paper is rapid, precise and of low cost, and it may be suitable for routine analysis. Further experiments suggest that this fluorescent probe can also detect other substances similar to catechol, for example, dopamine, levodopa, carbidopa, and epinephrine. In the present system, all of these substances can be oxidized by LAC, and the oxidized product can quench the fluorescence of NCDs (as shown in Fig. S7a, S7b, S7c, and S7d). In typical detection experiments, when dopamine, levodopa, carbidopa, or epinephrine is detected by the NCD–LAC hybrid, the PL intensity of NCDs changes linearly with increase (or decrease) in the concentrations of the above substances (principal test data for linear relationship, as shown in Table S2). The detection limit and detection range of the above substances are shown in Table 1 . Based on the above results, we can conclude that NCD–LAC-based florescence probes can serve as highly sensitive and reliable sensors for the detection of catechol as well as similar substances.

We developed a one-pot refluxing method for the fabrication of fluorescent NCDs from C3N4. The obtained NCDs had excellent aqueous dispersibility (without any surface modifications), and significantly, they exhibited stable and strong visible emission. These NCDs also possessed good proton adsorption capacity and photo-induced electron-accepting/donating abilities. Based on these unique properties, we demonstrated that the obtained NCDs can be used as a good fluorescent probe for cellular imaging. Further, the NCD–LAC hybrids were proved to be sensitive, stable, and of low cost for the precise detection of catechol and catechol derivatives. Our finding also suggested that the NCDs would serve as a promising candidate for a new type of fluorescence marker, optical imaging as well as high-efficiency catalyst design for applications in the fields of bioscience and energy technology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (2012CB825803, 2013CB932702), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51422207, 51132006, 21471106), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20123201110018), a Suzhou Planning Project of Science and Technology (ZXG2012028), and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.04.032.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This Supplementary material contains Figs. S1–S6 and Tables S1, S2 and S7.
